sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a non-healing sore on his right foot that he has had for about 2-3 weeks.

He initially developed a blister due to a new pair of shoes that caused neuropathy, but it progressed to a wound that has not improved despite using a band-aid and completing a course of antibiotics prescribed by his primary care physician.
He reports that the wound does not hurt and does not have any other symptoms such as fever, chills, or drainage.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with a last HbA1c of over 8, and has been referred to our office by his primary care physician.
Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms, but mentions that his neuropathy causes issues with stability.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's temperature is 98.1°F, heart rate is 72, respirations are 16, and blood pressure is 110/65.

Physical exam: The patient has a 1x2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, just proximal to the right fifth to fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, and no necrosis.
There is no odor, and no bony exposure.
The vascular exam shows palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, with no palpable DP or PT pulses, but with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer with no evidence of bone infection, requiring further evaluation and treatment.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Suspected peripheral artery disease; Diabetic neuropathy; Diabetes mellitus; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, to be covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
The patient will wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I do not think any more antibiotics are needed at this time.
I will see the patient again in 2 weeks to reassess the wound and potentially refer to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic consultant to help manage his diabetes and bring his HbA1c down.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in 2 weeks.
